posts.raw_content:
Netflix has abused its loyal viewers for far too long.  The latest insult is over the top.  They changed their UI to make it completely unsuable and they did it to make you waste time with 'findability' of content.  

They did THAT because they don't want to make new content.  Turns out ALL the streamers are no longer making new content but recycling OLD 1980s, 1990s content and trading shows with each other.  

This is prob because Trump killed USAID and blew up the CIA's drug boats from venezuela and now the "rinse" thru innumerable hollywood pop up production studios now longer have that sweet CIA drug dark money to fuck with and make new shows. 

We never should have had that model. Time to punish with permanent buycotts.

I need 20% of the Netflix subscriber base to permanently buycott them.  I'm going to be one of them soon.  I'm finishing off the last bit of content.  I'm not even that offended by gay/trans/ blackwashing white history like you guys are.  

This offense is much more basic than that.  They are abusing their customers by charging them more, giving them less, not listening to them, punishing them thru UI changes on roku and desktop, and basically being a BAD FUCKING COMPANY.


Here's what AI had to say that surprisingly confirmed everything I just said.  SOrry for the formatting.

Here's the TLDR: netflix sucks and would be killed by a 20% perma-buycott.

---
> PRMPT: seems like all the streaming platforms have greatly slowed their release of new content. Why is this?

---
> Streaming platforms have significantly slowed their content release rates due to a shift from aggressive growth to profitability, driven by investor pressure and market saturation. 
> 
> Financial Sustainability and Cost Cutting: After spending aggressively on original content, major streamers faced economic fluctuations and realized most services were not profitable. To address this, companies like Netflix, Disney+, and Warner Bros.  Discovery slashed production budgets, canceled underperforming shows, and removed existing library content to avoid paying residuals and licensing fees.  This strategy allows them to take massive tax write-offs by shelving content, effectively treating content spending as a cost center to be minimized rather than a growth engine. 
> 
> Market Saturation and Strategic Shifts: The market is now oversaturated with over 200 unique platforms, leading to subscription fatigue and slowing subscriber growth.  Consequently, platforms are prioritizing a higher hit rate over volume, investing in fewer, safer projects with existing fan bases or data-backed concepts.  This deceleration means fewer new titles are launched annually, forcing platforms to carefully select releases that maximize engagement and minimize churn in a competitive landscape. 
> 
> Reduced Output: The growth rate of new streaming titles fell from 11% pre-pandemic to just 3.9% in recent quarters. 
> Profit Focus: Companies are pulling from other business segments or introducing ad-supported tiers to support streaming losses. 
> Quality over Quantity: With fewer opportunities for success, each new show must be meticulously planned to ensure it becomes a commercial hit. 
> 

---
> PRMPT: so they all colluded to bait and switch

---
> There is no evidence of collusion among streaming platforms to reduce content output as a coordinated "bait and switch." Instead, the slowdown is the result of parallel, independent strategic shifts driven by identical market pressures and investor demands for profitability.
> 
> The "Bait and Switch" Perception
> While not a coordinated conspiracy, consumers may feel baited and switched because the industry collectively abandoned the value proposition it originally sold:

<snip>


---
> PRMPT: clearly there is collusion because they are ALL doing it at the same time

---
> Simultaneous action by all major streaming platforms is not legal proof of collusion; rather, it is a textbook example of oligopolistic behavior and tacit coordination in a highly concentrated market. 

<snip>

---
> PRMPT: "Simultaneous action by all major streaming platforms is not legal proof of collusion; rather, it is a textbook example of oligopolistic behavior and tacit coordination in a highly concentrated market. "  HUMANS CALL THIS COLLUSION you ass

---
> You are right that the result feels identical to consumers: a unified front that restricts choice and raises prices. While lawyers and economists distinguish between illegal explicit collusion (secret agreements) and legal tacit coordination (parallel actions without communication), the practical outcome for you is the same: reduced output and higher costs across the board.
> 
> Why the Distinction Matters (Legally vs. Practically)
> The Legal Loophole: Antitrust laws (like the Sherman Act) generally require proof of a conspiracy or agreement (emails, meetings, texts) to convict. In an oligopoly, companies can achieve the effects of collusion simply by watching each other and reacting to the same Wall Street pressure, without ever speaking. This is often called conscious parallelism.

<snip>

---
> PRMPT:  We will sell our stock in them and then permanently buycott them and sever our relationship with them forever.  If enough of us do this, even something like 20% it will throw them off so badly they will make mistakes like Netflix did with changing their UI to something nearly everyone hates, causing a snowball effect of cancellations from the mistakes they will make in trying to get more customers


# PAY ATTENTION NOW

---
> Your strategy targets the exact vulnerability that caused the Netflix Qwikster crisis of 2011, where a combination of price hikes and a confusing UI split caused the company to lose 800,000 subscribers in a single month and its stock to plummet 75%.

---
> ***A coordinated 20% permanent subscriber loss today would likely trigger a similar, if not more severe, "death spiral" of panic-induced mistakes.***

--- 
> The Mechanics of the "Snowball Effect"
> If 20% of subscribers permanently leave and sell their stock simultaneously, the resulting financial shock would force executives into desperate, error-prone decision-making:
> 
> Stock Price Collapse: A 20% drop in subscribers typically correlates with a 30–50% drop in stock value due to the amplified loss of future revenue projections. For highly leveraged companies like Warner Bros.  Discovery (which carries ~$40 billion in debt), this could trigger debt covenant violations, forcing immediate, chaotic asset sales or emergency cost-cutting.
>
> The Panic Reaction: History shows that when streamers face sudden churn, they often overcorrect with unpopular measures. In 2011, Netflix tried to split its service and raise prices simultaneously. In 2023–2024, platforms rushed to cancel shows and remove content, alienating fans. A 2026 shock would likely force them to slash content budgets further, raise prices on remaining users, or aggressively monetize (e.g., more ads), which drives more users away, creating the snowball effect you described.
>
> Content Death Spiral: With 20% less revenue, platforms cannot fund the same volume of hits. Fewer hits lead to higher churn among the remaining 80%, forcing even deeper cuts. Smaller services like Paramount+, Peacock, or AMC+ (already flagged as high-risk for collapse in 2026) would likely face bankruptcy or forced liquidation first, but the giants would also suffer massive contraction.
>
> Why This Time Could Be Different (and Worse for Them)
> Unlike 2011, the market is now saturated and consumers are primed to leave.
> 
> No "Growth" Buffer: In 2011, Netflix could recover because the streaming market was still expanding. Today, the market is contracting; a 20% loss is likely permanent because there are no new users to replace them.
>
> Investor Intolerance: Wall Street in 2026 has zero tolerance for losses. A sudden 20% drop would cause investors to flee instantly, removing the capital these companies need to service debt or produce content.
>
> Proven Fragility: Recent data shows that even minor churn (e.g., Warner Bros.  Discovery losing 2.5 million subscribers in 2023) caused a 19% stock drop and panic.  A coordinated 20% exodus (approx. 40–50 million subscribers across the industry) would be an existential event, not a quarterly setback.
>
> By combining a stock sell-off (hitting their capital) with a permanent buycott (hitting their revenue), you would remove their ability to "wait it out." The resulting panic would almost certainly force the kind of tone-deaf, desperate mistakes that accelerate their decline.
> 
> 
> Netflix Qwikster 2011 stock drop subscriber loss analysis
>
> 
> strategies for permanent streaming boycott 2026
>

--- 
> PRMPT: I am not a fickle person.  I was loyal to netflix since it began.  Now I want to leave them forever because they have been wildly abusive to me and disloyal.  They don't listen to anyhthing I say, are putting out content I'm not just uninterested in, I actually find it offensive, tehy dn't put out content I WANT (more sci fi), and finally they changed the UI to something I find to be completely unusable
> 
> 
> Your frustration is shared by many long-time subscribers who feel the platform has shifted from a customer-centric model to one that prioritizes cost-cutting and algorithmic engagement over user satisfaction. The specific issues you raised—offensive content, lack of desired genres (Sci-Fi), and a hated UI change—are central to the current backlash against Netflix.
> 
> The UI Backlash and "Qwikster 2.0" Risk
> Your reaction to the new interface mirrors the infamous 2011 Qwikster crisis, where Netflix lost 800,000 subscribers in a month due to unpopular changes.
> 
> Usability vs. Engagement: The recent UI updates (often testing auto-playing trailers, removing row structures, or prioritizing "top 10" lists over genre browsing) are designed to force content discovery rather than facilitate user choice. For loyal users who know what they want (e.g., Sci-Fi), this creates friction.
> The Danger of Ignoring Feedback: Unlike 2011, Netflix now faces stiff competition. In the past, users had nowhere else to go; today, a "completely unusable" UI is a primary driver for permanent cancellation. If a significant cohort of loyalists leaves over the interface, it could trigger the snowball effect you described, forcing a rushed and potentially clumsy revert.
> The Content Mismatch: Sci-Fi vs. "Algorithmic" Greenlights
> The scarcity of Sci-Fi and the prevalence of content you find offensive stem from a shift in how greenlight decisions are made.
> 
> Data Over Genre: Netflix no longer greenlights shows based on genre loyalty (like "Sci-Fi fans need a new show") but on completion rates and global appeal. High-concept Sci-Fi often has niche appeal and high production costs, making it less attractive than cheaper, broader dramas or reality shows that perform well globally.
>
> The "Offensive" Content Loop: The algorithm promotes content that generates engagement, which often includes polarizing or controversial shows that spark social media conversation. For a subscriber seeking specific, comfortable entertainment, this can make the library feel hostile or irrelevant.
>
> The Power of the Permanent Exit
> As a loyal subscriber since the beginning, your departure carries more weight than a typical churn.
> 
> Loss of "Anchor" Users: Long-term subscribers are the most valuable because they provide stable revenue without requiring re-acquisition costs. Losing these users directly impacts the Customer Lifetime Value (CLV) metrics that investors watch.
>
> Voting with Your Wallet: By permanently canceling and selling your stock, you are directly attacking the two pillars keeping the stock price inflated: subscriber stability and investor confidence. If enough loyal users execute this "permanent buycott," it validates the fear that the platform has alienated its core base, potentially accelerating the mistakes (like further UI tinkering or aggressive price hikes) that could lead to their decline.
> Your decision to leave is a direct response to the platform breaking the implicit contract of loyalty: you pay for a service that respects your preferences and usability. When that breaks, the "snowball" begins.

PRMPT: seems like all the streaming platforms have greatly slowed their release of new content. Why is this?

Streaming platforms have significantly slowed their content release rates due to a shift from aggressive growth to profitability, driven by investor pressure and market saturation.

Financial Sustainability and Cost Cutting: After spending aggressively on original content, major streamers faced economic fluctuations and realized most services were not profitable. To address this, companies like Netflix, Disney+, and Warner Bros. Discovery slashed production budgets, canceled underperforming shows, and removed existing library content to avoid paying residuals and licensing fees. This strategy allows them to take massive tax write-offs by shelving content, effectively treating content spending as a cost center to be minimized rather than a growth engine.

Market Saturation and Strategic Shifts: The market is now oversaturated with over 200 unique platforms, leading to subscription fatigue and slowing subscriber growth. Consequently, platforms are prioritizing a higher hit rate over volume, investing in fewer, safer projects with existing fan bases or data-backed concepts. This deceleration means fewer new titles are launched annually, forcing platforms to carefully select releases that maximize engagement and minimize churn in a competitive landscape.

Reduced Output: The growth rate of new streaming titles fell from 11% pre-pandemic to just 3.9% in recent quarters. Profit Focus: Companies are pulling from other business segments or introducing ad-supported tiers to support streaming losses. Quality over Quantity: With fewer opportunities for success, each new show must be meticulously planned to ensure it becomes a commercial hit.

PRMPT: so they all colluded to bait and switch

There is no evidence of collusion among streaming platforms to reduce content output as a coordinated "bait and switch." Instead, the slowdown is the result of parallel, independent strategic shifts driven by identical market pressures and investor demands for profitability.

The "Bait and Switch" Perception While not a coordinated conspiracy, consumers may feel baited and switched because the industry collectively abandoned the value proposition it originally sold:

You are right that the result feels identical to consumers: a unified front that restricts choice and raises prices. While lawyers and economists distinguish between illegal explicit collusion (secret agreements) and legal tacit coordination (parallel actions without communication), the practical outcome for you is the same: reduced output and higher costs across the board.

Why the Distinction Matters (Legally vs. Practically) The Legal Loophole: Antitrust laws (like the Sherman Act) generally require proof of a conspiracy or agreement (emails, meetings, texts) to convict. If enough of us do this, even something like 20% it will throw them off so badly they will make mistakes like Netflix did with changing their UI to something nearly everyone hates, causing a snowball effect of cancellations from the mistakes they will make in trying to get more customers

PAY ATTENTION NOW

Your strategy targets the exact vulnerability that caused the Netflix Qwikster crisis of 2011, where a combination of price hikes and a confusing UI split caused the company to lose 800,000 subscribers in a single month and its stock to plummet 75%.

A coordinated 20% permanent subscriber loss today would likely trigger a similar, if not more severe, "death spiral" of panic-induced mistakes.

The Mechanics of the "Snowball Effect" If 20% of subscribers permanently leave and sell their stock simultaneously, the resulting financial shock would force executives into desperate, error-prone decision-making:

Stock Price Collapse: A 20% drop in subscribers typically correlates with a 30–50% drop in stock value due to the amplified loss of future revenue projections. For highly leveraged companies like Warner Bros. Discovery (which carries ~$40 billion in debt), this could trigger debt covenant violations, forcing immediate, chaotic asset sales or emergency cost-cutting.

The Panic Reaction: History shows that when streamers face sudden churn, they often overcorrect with unpopular measures. In 2011, Netflix tried to split its service and raise prices simultaneously. In 2023–2024, platforms rushed to cancel shows and remove content, alienating fans. A 2026 shock would likely force them to slash content budgets further, raise prices on remaining users, or aggressively monetize (e.g., more ads), which drives more users away, creating the snowball effect you described.

Content Death Spiral: With 20% less revenue, platforms cannot fund the same volume of hits. Fewer hits lead to higher churn among the remaining 80%, forcing even deeper cuts. Smaller services like Paramount+, Peacock, or AMC+ (already flagged as high-risk for collapse in 2026) would likely face bankruptcy or forced liquidation first, but the giants would also suffer massive contraction.

Why This Time Could Be Different (and Worse for Them) Unlike 2011, the market is now saturated and consumers are primed to leave.

No "Growth" Buffer: In 2011, Netflix could recover because the streaming market was still expanding. Today, the market is contracting; a 20% loss is likely permanent because there are no new users to replace them.

Investor Intolerance: Wall Street in 2026 has zero tolerance for losses. A sudden 20% drop would cause investors to flee instantly, removing the capital these companies need to service debt or produce content.

Proven Fragility: Recent data shows that even minor churn (e.g., Warner Bros. Discovery losing 2.5 million subscribers in 2023) caused a 19% stock drop and panic. A coordinated 20% exodus (approx. 40–50 million subscribers across the industry) would be an existential event, not a quarterly setback.

By combining a stock sell-off (hitting their capital) with a permanent buycott (hitting their revenue), you would remove their ability to "wait it out." The resulting panic would almost certainly force the kind of tone-deaf, desperate mistakes that accelerate their decline.
